reflection_text: In class today, we focused on high-alert medications as defined by ISMP — specifically the top 10 list including insulin, anticoagulants such as warfarin and heparin, opioids, concentrated electrolytes like potassium chloride, and chemotherapy agents. We discussed how these drugs require additional safeguards not because errors are more frequent, but because the consequences are more devastating when they do occur. We also reviewed specific strategies like independent double checks, standardized concentrations, and removing concentrated potassium from floor stock. This topic is critically important to me because it challenges my prior assumption that all medications carry equal risk. I had not fully appreciated how certain drugs — if given at the wrong dose or to the wrong patient — can cause immediate, irreversible harm. The ISMP framework gave me a more nuanced understanding of risk stratification, which I believe will fundamentally change how I approach dispensing. I was also struck by how systemic changes — like removing high-alert medications from unit stock — can be more effective than relying on individual vigilance alone. During my upcoming internship rotation at a hospital pharmacy, I will create a personal reference card listing the site-specific high-alert medications and the required double-check protocols. I will also ask my preceptor to walk me through a real example of how an independent double check is performed at that site, so I understand the workflow rather than just the concept.

Today's class covered the ISMP high-alert medication list and the rationale for why specific categories of drugs — particularly concentrated electrolytes, insulin, anticoagulants, opioids, and neuromuscular blocking agents — warrant enhanced safeguards. We reviewed case studies involving patients who received 10-fold insulin overdoses or potassium chloride IV pushes and the fatal outcomes that followed. We also discussed the difference between error prevention (stopping mistakes) and harm reduction (minimizing impact when errors occur). Understanding high-alert medications is fundamental to my development as a pharmacist because it shifts my mindset from general carefulness to targeted vigilance. The cases we reviewed showed that errors involving these drugs often follow predictable patterns — look-alike packaging, unclear labeling, unit-of-measure confusion — which means they are largely preventable through system design. Knowing which drugs are on this list will help me prioritize where to focus my attention. In the future, I will try to be more careful when handling these medications and make sure I follow safety protocols at my internship site.

This week's session introduced the concept of high-alert medications, including the ISMP classification system and the specific categories most commonly associated with serious patient harm. We reviewed strategies like tall man lettering, physical separation on shelves, and limit-access storage for drugs like concentrated potassium chloride and neuromuscular blocking agents. We also practiced identifying high-alert status using the ISMP website. Patient safety is a core part of pharmacy practice and knowing about high-alert medications is important for preventing harm. These are drugs that we will encounter regularly, and I know I should be more careful with them than others. When I start my clinical rotations, I will ask my preceptor on the first day to show me where high-alert medications are stored, what the site-specific safeguards are, and whether I am allowed to participate in independent double checks. I will also set aside time each week to review one ISMP alert or case report to build my knowledge of current safety issues.

We covered the topic of high-alert medications in class and learned about how they are categorized. The class also reviewed some safety strategies that hospitals and pharmacies use. The details of specific drug categories were discussed as examples. This topic matters deeply to me personally because of an experience I had during shadowing, where I saw a nurse almost give an undiluted potassium chloride injection because it was stored adjacent to a saline flush. The patient was fortunately not harmed, but that moment stayed with me. It made me realize how real these risks are — not abstract, not just in textbooks. The system had failed that nurse before she even touched the vial. During my internship, I plan to proactively audit the drug storage areas I work in, focusing specifically on where high-alert medications are kept relative to similar-looking or similarly-packaged drugs. If I find anything concerning, I will document it using the site's safety reporting system and bring it to my preceptor.

In today's session, we went through the ISMP high-alert medication list in detail, including the top-tier categories such as concentrated electrolytes, insulin, anticoagulants, and chemotherapy. We reviewed how errors with these medications differ in impact rather than frequency, and why extra safeguards are justified. Case examples involving insulin dosing confusion between U-100 and U-500 formulations were particularly memorable. High-alert medications are important to know about as a pharmacist because they carry serious risk of patient harm. It is part of our responsibility to make sure these drugs are handled carefully. I want to make sure I remember this going forward in my career.

In class we discussed the topic of high-alert medications and how they are different from other drugs. We learned about the ISMP list and some examples of medications that require extra care. Safety is important in pharmacy and these medications can cause significant harm if used incorrectly. I will keep this in mind as I continue my training and try to apply these safety principles at my internship.

Today's class was about medication safety. We talked about some types of drugs that are particularly risky. Patient safety is a key responsibility for pharmacists. I will try to do my best when working with any medications.
Today we covered an important pharmacy topic. I learned some things about how drugs can be dangerous. I will try to remember this for my career.

In today's class, we did a deep dive into look-alike/sound-alike (LASA) drugs, focusing on the cognitive mechanisms behind LASA errors — specifically confirmation bias, top-down processing, and the speed-accuracy tradeoff in busy dispensing environments. We reviewed real ISMP error reports involving confusion between hydromorphone and morphine, vinCRIStine and vinBLAStine, and clonidine and clonazepam. We also learned about systematic safeguards including tall man lettering, barcode medication administration (BCMA), visual alerts in pharmacy software, and physical separation on shelving. This session challenged my assumption that LASA errors are primarily caused by inattention or individual carelessness. Instead, I now understand that they are largely driven by predictable cognitive shortcuts that everyone uses — making systemic safeguards essential rather than optional. As someone who has worked in a retail pharmacy where multiple generic formulations of similar drugs are shelved together, I can now see exactly how these errors happen. I was also struck by the fact that LASA errors persist even with electronic systems, because barcode scanning is sometimes bypassed under time pressure. During my next internship rotation, I will specifically observe how LASA medications are stored and labeled at the site, and ask my preceptor about the most common LASA pairs encountered there. I will create a site-specific LASA reference and use it during every dispensing verification step.

Today we studied look-alike/sound-alike medications, reviewing the ISMP list of high-risk LASA pairs and the specific mechanisms by which these errors occur in dispensing, transcription, and administration. We discussed how tall man lettering works by emphasizing letter differences to slow down reading and reduce confirmation bias — for example, DOBUTamine vs. DOPamine. We also examined cases where sound-alike drug names caused errors during verbal orders, such as between Celebrex and Celexa or between Xanax and Zantac. Understanding LASA medications is central to safe pharmacy practice because it shows that even well-trained professionals can make mistakes when the environment is designed in a way that exploits normal cognitive patterns. This is not about individual failure — it is about system design. Learning about tall man lettering and barcode systems showed me that clever, simple interventions can meaningfully reduce error rates without relying on individual heroics. I will pay attention to LASA pairs in my future practice and try to use the safeguards that are in place.

This week's class covered look-alike/sound-alike drug errors in depth, including the use of ISMP's LASA drug list, the role of pharmacy software in generating LASA warnings, and case studies of patient harm caused by LASA mix-ups. We specifically reviewed the case involving vinCRIStine given intrathecally instead of intravenously, resulting in patient death, and discussed what system failures allowed this to happen. LASA errors are an important topic for pharmacists to understand. Many of these errors have resulted in patient harm, and knowing about them is part of our professional responsibility. I will apply this knowledge by memorizing the most critical LASA pairs on the ISMP list and setting them as pop-up alerts on whatever pharmacy system I use during rotations. I also plan to speak with my preceptor about what verbal order protocols are in place at the site to prevent sound-alike confusion, and observe how these are handled in practice.

We discussed look-alike and sound-alike drugs in class today and reviewed some examples of errors that have occurred. We also talked about strategies for preventing these types of mistakes. This topic resonated with me because during a community pharmacy internship last summer, I nearly reached for amlodipine 10 mg instead of amiloride 5 mg during a busy Saturday shift. The labels were similar in color and font, and the drug names shared several letters. I caught the error before dispensing, but it made me realize how easily this could happen even to someone who was being careful. That experience, combined with what I learned in class about confirmation bias, changed how I think about my own cognitive vulnerabilities. I will be mindful of LASA pairs at my future sites and follow available protocols.

In today's session, we covered the topic of look-alike/sound-alike medications, including specific ISMP-identified pairs and the strategies used to prevent errors — such as tall man lettering, physical separation, and electronic alerts. We also reviewed examples of mix-ups between oral medications with similar-looking tablets and between IV medications with similar vial sizes. I am still developing my understanding of why this topic matters specifically to me as a future pharmacist, but I recognize that these errors are documented and cause harm. I will try to pay attention to LASA medications at my internship.

We went over some medication mix-up topics in class. Understanding LASA errors is something I feel strongly about because I once witnessed my father receive the wrong medication at a retail pharmacy — a drug that sounded similar to his actual prescription. He noticed only because the pill color was different. If that visual cue had not been there, he would have taken the wrong drug for weeks. That experience made me want to become a pharmacist who actively works to prevent these errors. I plan to specifically ask my preceptor at my upcoming rotation about the LASA protocols at their site. I will also review the ISMP LASA list monthly and flag any pairs I encounter in practice.

In today's class, we talked about look-alike and sound-alike medications and some of the errors that can result. There were some interesting examples shared. This topic is important because pharmacists have a responsibility to keep patients safe, and LASA errors can cause real harm. I should keep this in mind as I continue my training.
We had class today about medications and how they can get mixed up. I will pay more attention to drug names when I am at my internship and make sure to slow down.

Today we covered medication reconciliation — the process of comparing a patient's current medication list against new orders at transitions of care such as admission, transfer between units, and discharge. We reviewed the Joint Commission requirements for reconciliation and the types of errors it is designed to prevent: omissions, duplications, dosing discrepancies, and drug interactions introduced during transitions. We worked through a case of a patient admitted for a hip fracture whose metoprolol was omitted during hospital admission, leading to a hypertensive crisis postoperatively. This topic directly shifted my understanding of where medication errors originate. I previously assumed that most errors happened in the dispensing step, but this session showed me that the handoff between care settings is an extremely vulnerable point. Patients often manage multiple medications from multiple prescribers, and the list they carry can be inconsistent, outdated, or incomplete. As a pharmacist, I am well-positioned to catch these discrepancies because I have access to dispensing history, patient profiles, and prescribing records — a perspective that nurses and physicians may not have. During my hospital rotation, I will ask to participate in the medication reconciliation process at admission and discharge for at least one patient per shift. I will practice building a complete best possible medication history by interviewing the patient, consulting dispensing records, and contacting the patient's home pharmacy.

In class today, we learned about the medication reconciliation process, including its three main steps: verification (obtaining a complete list), clarification (checking that orders are appropriate), and reconciliation (resolving discrepancies). We reviewed tools like the BPMH (best possible medication history) and discussed common pitfalls such as patients not knowing their medication names or doses. We also watched a video simulation of a pharmacist conducting a reconciliation interview at hospital admission. Medication reconciliation is clearly an important patient safety process, and pharmacists are well-suited to lead it given their medication expertise. I understand this is a high-error zone in healthcare. When I start my inpatient rotation, I will use the standardized reconciliation form at the site and practice completing a BPMH by interviewing at least three patients during the first week. I will track any discrepancies I find and review them with my preceptor to understand how they are handled.

We discussed medication reconciliation in class today, including how errors can happen during transitions of care. We reviewed some case examples and tools used in the process. I appreciate the depth of this topic now after hearing a patient story in class. A patient who was admitted to the hospital for cardiac surgery was not asked about their supplement use during reconciliation, and their fish oil combined with the anticoagulant they were started on post-op contributed to a significant bleed. I had not thought about how over-the-counter products and supplements would be part of my reconciliation responsibility, and this case challenged me to think more comprehensively about what a complete medication list actually means. I will always include OTC drugs, vitamins, and supplements in any medication list I collect from patients. During my upcoming rotation, I will ask to observe a pharmacist-led reconciliation interview and take notes on how open-ended questions are used to elicit a complete history.

Today's class gave a thorough overview of medication reconciliation, focusing on the transitions of care model — what happens to a patient's medications at admission, throughout hospitalization, and at discharge. We reviewed Joint Commission standards, specific error types (omissions, dose discrepancies, therapeutic duplications), and the role of the clinical pharmacist versus pharmacy technician in the reconciliation workflow. We also discussed evidence showing that pharmacist-led reconciliation significantly reduces discrepancy rates. This topic matters because medication reconciliation is one of the most evidence-backed interventions for preventing adverse drug events at transitions of care. I now understand that the hospital-to-home transition in particular is extremely high-risk — patients often go home with a confusing bag of medication bottles, instructions from multiple providers, and no clear guidance on what changed during their stay. The pharmacist is often the last and best line of defense before the patient walks out the door. I am not yet sure exactly how I will apply this in practice, but I know it is something I need to take seriously.

We covered medication reconciliation today and discussed how pharmacists play an important role in verifying medication lists when patients are admitted or discharged from the hospital. We saw some examples of errors that resulted from incomplete reconciliation. Medication reconciliation is an important process because patients often have complex medication regimens, and errors during transitions of care can cause harm. Pharmacists are in a good position to help catch these errors. I will try to apply what I learned when I have the opportunity during my rotations.

Today's class introduced a new topic related to medication management. I honestly found the material a bit dense and I am still working to understand all the components. However, I felt strongly connected to the topic because my grandfather was recently hospitalized and discharged with a medication list that nobody explained to him or my family. He ended up taking both his old and new blood pressure medications at the same time for three days because he did not know to stop the old one. This situation showed me how real and preventable these problems are. I want to learn how to do medication reconciliation well and will ask my preceptor to let me practice this skill during my hospital rotation.

We talked about medication reconciliation and its role in pharmacy practice. There were some case examples discussed in class. I plan to pay more attention to this at my internship when I have the chance.
Class today covered a medication safety topic. I will look for opportunities to practice what we discussed at my internship site and ask questions if I am unsure.

In class today, we did an in-depth review of pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic drug-drug interactions, focusing on the CYP450 enzyme system. We specifically examined CYP3A4 inhibitors and inducers — like clarithromycin, fluconazole, rifampin, and St. John's Wort — and their effects on drugs like cyclosporine, warfarin, and simvastatin. We also covered QTc-prolonging drug combinations and the additive risk of combining agents such as azithromycin, haloperidol, and methadone in a single patient. One case study examined a transplant patient who started taking a grapefruit supplement and experienced calcineurin inhibitor toxicity. This session fundamentally changed how I approach medication review. I previously viewed drug interactions as rare edge cases to look up when triggered by an alert. Now I understand they are a predictable result of how drugs are metabolized and that knowing the underlying mechanisms allows me to anticipate interactions before they occur — without relying solely on software. This is especially relevant for complex patients on many medications, where alerts may fire so frequently that clinicians experience alert fatigue. I will apply this learning by selecting two to three CYP450 interactions per week to study in depth during my rotations, using real patient cases. I will also make it a habit to review QTc risk with my preceptor whenever a patient is on more than one QTc-prolonging agent.

Today we covered the pharmacology of drug-drug interactions, with a focus on CYP450-mediated metabolism and transporter-based interactions (P-glycoprotein). We reviewed the clinical significance of interactions using the Lexicomp and Micromedex rating systems and discussed how to triage which interactions require immediate action versus monitoring. We worked through cases involving warfarin combined with antibiotics and NSAIDs, and the interaction between statins and fibrates causing myopathy. This topic is particularly relevant to me because I already work as a pharmacy technician in a busy retail pharmacy, and I frequently see patients on multiple medications who are never counseled about interactions. I have observed pharmacists dismiss interaction alerts rapidly under time pressure, and now I understand how dangerous that can be — especially for drugs with narrow therapeutic indices. I want to be the kind of pharmacist who can quickly assess the clinical significance of an interaction rather than reflexively overriding alerts. I will aim to improve my interaction counseling skills during my rotations.

This session focused on drug-drug interactions, covering both pharmacokinetic interactions (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion) and pharmacodynamic interactions (synergistic, additive, antagonistic effects). We reviewed the serotonin syndrome triad — cognitive changes, autonomic dysfunction, and neuromuscular abnormality — and which drug combinations most commonly precipitate it, including SSRIs with tramadol or triptans. We also discussed the clinical management of interactions, including dose adjustments, timing separations, and therapeutic substitutions. Drug interactions are important, and pharmacists need to be aware of them. I plan to use the interaction screening tools available at my site systematically during medication reviews and document any significant interactions I identify. I will also specifically look for patterns related to serotonin syndrome risk in patients on psychiatric medications combined with other drugs.

We reviewed different types of drug-drug interactions in today's class, including some of the most clinically significant ones that pharmacists encounter. There were many examples, and I am still working to organize them in my mind. This topic hits close to home because my mother was briefly hospitalized after a drug interaction between her antibiotic and blood thinner was not caught at the pharmacy. The pharmacist later told us it was a known interaction, which made the incident feel preventable. That experience is part of why I chose pharmacy, and it motivates me to be rigorous about interaction screening. I will make sure to review all interaction alerts carefully at my site rather than dismissing them, and will ask my preceptor to help me develop a triage framework.

In today's class, we covered CYP450 drug interactions with a focus on the most clinically relevant inhibitors and inducers. We reviewed specific drug pairs — fluconazole with cyclosporine, rifampin with oral contraceptives, and warfarin with amiodarone — and how these interactions are identified, classified, and managed. We used Lexicomp to assess interaction severity and worked through clinical recommendations for each case. I do not yet feel a strong personal connection to this topic beyond understanding it is part of pharmacist competency. I will review interaction databases at my internship when relevant.

We discussed drug interactions in class, including types of pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic interactions and why they matter for patient safety. We went through several examples of harmful interactions that have occurred in clinical practice. Drug interactions are a key part of pharmacy practice and I need to understand them to protect patients. I will use the resources available at my internship site to screen for interactions and ask for guidance when I am unsure.
Today we talked about interactions between medications. It seems like there are many combinations that can be dangerous. I know this is something pharmacists check for and I will pay attention to it.
Class today covered some drug topics. I will work on learning more about the medications I encounter and use the tools available to me to check for any problems.

Today's class focused on anticoagulation safety, a topic I had studied pharmacologically before but not from a patient safety perspective. We reviewed the specific risks associated with the four main anticoagulant classes — heparin (unfractionated and LMWH), warfarin, and direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) — and why they appear consistently on ISMP's high-alert medication list. We discussed the importance of INR monitoring, dose adjustment strategies, bridging therapy protocols, and reversal agents (vitamin K, 4-factor PCC, idarucizumab, andexanet alfa). We also reviewed cases of patients who received therapeutic heparin boluses without dose-weight adjustment, resulting in serious bleeds. This session expanded my understanding of anticoagulation from a clinical pharmacology topic to a systems safety topic. I realized that errors with anticoagulants are often the result of multiple system vulnerabilities converging — unclear weight-based dosing protocols, absent double-check requirements, and inadequate monitoring frequencies. I was also struck by how the introduction of DOACs, which were marketed as safer and requiring less monitoring, has actually introduced new safety concerns because patients and providers may underestimate their risks. At my upcoming internal medicine rotation, I will ask to be assigned to at least one anticoagulation patient each week so I can practice dose management, INR interpretation, and patient counseling about signs of bleeding and when to seek care.

In class, we covered the full spectrum of anticoagulation safety, including the pharmacokinetics of heparin, LMWH, warfarin, and DOACs; monitoring parameters; and the management of supratherapeutic anticoagulation. We reviewed case reports of patients who were started on warfarin without a concurrent bridging protocol and developed thromboembolic events while awaiting therapeutic INR levels. We also analyzed errors involving anti-Xa level interpretation for heparin dosing. Anticoagulation management is a core pharmacist competency, and the safety issues are genuinely complex. This class helped me see that errors in anticoagulation are rarely simple — they involve interactions between dosing algorithms, monitoring schedules, communication among the care team, and patient adherence. As someone interested in hospital pharmacy, I need to be expert-level in this area. I will develop a personal anticoagulation reference sheet and ask to observe the anticoagulation pharmacist at my rotation site.

We reviewed anticoagulation safety in class today, including common errors and the safeguards used to prevent them. There were several case studies shared. My grandmother is currently on warfarin for atrial fibrillation, and hearing about the number of serious bleeds caused by poorly managed anticoagulation was alarming. I have seen her INR fluctuate significantly based on what she eats and what supplements she takes, and this class made me realize how vulnerable patients on warfarin are if they do not have consistent pharmacist support. I will make it a point during my rotations to practice INR counseling and patient education about dietary interactions with warfarin. I also want to understand DOAC protocols well enough to counsel patients on the importance of adherence even when they feel well.

Today's class covered anticoagulation safety from a comprehensive standpoint, reviewing each drug class, monitoring requirements, and the case for and against different anticoagulation strategies. We also discussed the evidence behind pharmacist-led anticoagulation clinics and their impact on reducing adverse events and hospitalizations for patients on warfarin. The session reinforced my understanding of the risks involved and the pharmacist's key role in monitoring. I plan to focus on anticoagulation education at my rotation and ask to be involved in patient monitoring activities.

Today's session covered anticoagulants as high-alert medications in depth, including specific dosing protocols for UFH, LMWH, and warfarin, reversal strategies, and monitoring parameters like aPTT, anti-Xa levels, and INR. We reviewed examples of heparin protocol deviations that resulted in serious patient harm. I am still connecting this topic to my personal professional motivations, but I understand its importance. I will focus on anticoagulation cases during my upcoming clinical rotation.

We learned about anticoagulant medications and the safety risks associated with them. These include drugs like warfarin and heparin, which are commonly used in hospital settings. Anticoagulants are high-alert medications with serious risks, and pharmacists have an important role in monitoring patients who take them. I will try to be diligent when working with these drugs in practice.

Today we talked about some types of blood thinners and their safety considerations. Anticoagulants are important medications that require careful monitoring. I will remember to be careful with these drugs.
We covered anticoagulation in class today. I learned about some important medications and their risks.

This week's session focused on pediatric dosing safety, covering weight-based and age-based dosing calculations, the risks of 10-fold dosing errors, and the importance of always questioning doses that fall outside expected ranges. We reviewed the Broselow tape as a quick reference tool and discussed why pediatric patients cannot simply receive scaled-down adult doses. We also analyzed a sentinel event where an infant received a morphine dose appropriate for a 70 kg adult, resulting in respiratory arrest. I came into this class thinking I was fairly confident in my dosing calculation skills, but the examples showed me how easy it is to make a 10-fold error with a misplaced decimal point — and how fatal the consequences can be in a pediatric patient with limited physiological reserve. I had not considered how much the absence of commercially available pediatric formulations drives compounding errors, or how weight-based dosing can be anchored to an incorrect weight if the patient was weighed in the wrong unit or position. This class genuinely changed my vigilance level. At my pediatric rotation, I will double-check every dose I prepare or verify by (1) confirming the weight used for calculation and the units, (2) calculating the mg/kg dose independently, (3) comparing it against the expected range in a reference like Lexi-Pediatrics, and (4) asking a second pharmacist to verify before dispensing.

Today we covered pediatric pharmacokinetics and the principles behind weight-based and body-surface-area-based dosing. We reviewed common dosing errors in pediatrics, including decimal point errors, unit confusions (mg vs. mcg), and the use of incorrect weight references. We discussed why children metabolize and respond to drugs differently at different developmental stages, and why extrapolating adult data to pediatrics is dangerous. Understanding pediatric dosing safety is especially important to me because I shadowed in a pediatric emergency department last summer and observed how rapidly small patients can deteriorate if given incorrect doses. I saw a child given acetaminophen based on weight in pounds when the formula required weight in kilograms, resulting in a dose that was more than double the correct amount. The error was caught before harm, but the experience was unsettling. I am still thinking about what specific changes I will make to my practice, but I know I need to be more structured about verifying weights and units.

We discussed pediatric dosing in class today, reviewing calculation methods, common errors, and safety strategies. The instructor mentioned several real-world cases involving decimal errors. I feel particularly motivated to understand pediatric dosing because I want to work in a children's hospital setting after graduation. The cases shared today made me realize that pediatric pharmacy requires an extraordinarily high level of attention to detail — not just because children are physiologically different, but because the consequences of even a small dosing error are proportionally much more severe due to smaller body mass and immature organ function. During my pediatric rotation, I will ask my preceptor to walk me through the site's pediatric dosing protocols and error-prevention procedures. I will also practice performing two independent dose calculations for each pediatric order I encounter before verifying it.

In today's class, we reviewed pediatric dosing principles including weight-based calculations, developmental pharmacokinetics, and the importance of oral formulation availability. We looked at real-world cases where lack of pediatric-specific formulations led to compounding errors or adult dosage form manipulation. This is an important topic for pharmacists working with pediatric patients. Errors in this population can be especially serious due to physiological differences. I will keep this in mind for future practice.

We covered pediatric dosing safety today, including common error types and how to prevent them. We talked about weight-based dosing, unit errors, and formulation challenges. Pediatric patients are a vulnerable population, and pharmacists have a responsibility to ensure accurate dosing. I will be careful and thorough when verifying pediatric orders during my training.
Today's class was about dosing in children. It seems like this is an area where errors can happen easily. Pediatric pharmacy requires careful attention to detail and I will keep this in mind.
We talked about pediatric medications today. I will practice my dosing calculations and review any pediatric orders carefully when I am at my rotation site.

This class covered controlled substance management, including DEA scheduling, the regulatory requirements for prescribing and dispensing, and pharmacy-specific responsibilities around inventory, record-keeping, and disposal. We reviewed the prescription drug monitoring program (PDMP) and how it serves as both a clinical and regulatory tool to identify patients at risk for opioid misuse and diversion. We also discussed common diversion scenarios in pharmacy settings and the internal controls designed to detect them, including perpetual inventory systems, DEA Form 222, and the CURES database in California. This session deepened my understanding of the pharmacist's dual role as clinician and compliance officer in controlled substance management. I had previously thought of PDMP checks as a regulatory formality, but after reviewing cases where pharmacists had legal exposure for filling prescriptions for patients with obvious doctor-shopping patterns, I now understand that the PDMP is a genuinely important clinical tool. I was also struck by how workplace diversion — often by trusted colleagues — is more common than we assume and that pharmacists have a responsibility to create and maintain internal safeguards. At my community pharmacy internship, I will ask my preceptor to show me how PDMP checks are performed at the site, including what criteria are used to determine when a prescription raises concerns. I will also request access to observe at least one inventory count procedure and ask about how discrepancies are investigated.

Today we went through the DEA scheduling system, controlled substance dispensing requirements, the pharmacy's role in identifying prescription fraud and diversion, and state-level prescription drug monitoring programs. We worked through case studies of pharmacists who failed to exercise corresponding responsibility when filling clearly suspicious prescriptions and the legal consequences that followed. We also reviewed disposal options for controlled substances and the DEA take-back program. Controlled substances are clearly important to manage properly. Pharmacists have legal responsibility for appropriate dispensing, and I understand I will need to apply this consistently in practice. When I rotate through a community pharmacy, I will practice conducting PDMP checks for every Schedule II prescription I process and document my reasoning. I will also ask my preceptor about how the site has handled a suspicious prescription in the past so I understand the workflow.

Class today covered controlled substance regulations and the pharmacist's responsibilities, including dispensing requirements, PDMP use, and inventory controls. We saw some case examples of diversion and fraudulent prescribing. I feel strongly about this topic because I had a distressing experience at a prior internship where I noticed a technician seemed to be removing medications without documentation. I was unsure what to do and ultimately said nothing, which I still regret. This class gave me the vocabulary and regulatory framework to understand what diversion looks like, what the pharmacy's responsibility is, and what reporting mechanisms exist. I now know that staying silent would make me complicit. I will speak up if I observe something suspicious at my future sites and make sure to know the reporting procedures before my next rotation starts.

In class today, we reviewed the DEA scheduling framework, the requirements for dispensing Schedule II-V substances, documentation requirements, and the role of the PDMP in preventing misuse. We discussed what constitutes a legitimate medical purpose and how pharmacists exercise professional judgment when filling controlled substance prescriptions. Controlled substances are high-risk medications with significant abuse potential, and pharmacists have an important gatekeeping role. I will keep these responsibilities in mind as I continue my training.

We discussed controlled substances in class today, including DEA schedules, dispensing rules, and the role of the PDMP. We also talked about diversion and what pharmacists should do if they suspect it is occurring. Controlled substance management is a critical part of pharmacy practice with both patient safety and legal dimensions. I will try to follow all regulations carefully and use the PDMP as part of my dispensing workflow.

Today we went over some rules and regulations around controlled drugs. This is clearly an important area for pharmacists to know well since it involves both patient care and the law. I will ask my preceptor about the processes used at my site.
Class today was about controlled substances and their scheduling. There is a lot to know about this topic and I will continue learning.

Today's class covered pharmaceutical compounding safety, with particular emphasis on USP 795 (non-sterile) and USP 797 (sterile) standards and the regulatory context created by the Drug Quality and Security Act following the 2012 New England Compounding Center meningitis outbreak that killed 64 patients. We reviewed the key principles of compounding safety: beyond-use dating, environmental monitoring requirements, personnel training and competency, equipment calibration, and quality control testing. We also discussed how compounding pharmacies are regulated differently from manufacturers and the ongoing debate about oversight gaps. The 2012 outbreak shocked me in a way that reading about it in isolation would not have. Learning about the specific failures — contaminated sterile compounding, inadequate testing, regulatory oversight gaps — and seeing the scale of harm that resulted made the subject of compounding standards feel urgent and personal. I now understand that USP standards are not just regulatory checkboxes but the minimum safeguards standing between a patient and a potentially fatal contamination. The fact that these standards are still being phased in and resisted in some settings was disturbing. During any compounding rotation, I will actively practice USP 797 technique including garbing, aseptic manipulation, and environmental monitoring procedures. I will also read the current status of USP 800 for hazardous drug handling and ask my preceptor how the site is prepared for compliance.

In class today we covered the regulatory and safety framework for pharmaceutical compounding, including the historical context of the DQSA, the distinction between 503A and 503B compounders, and the specific requirements for sterile compounding under USP 797. We reviewed the contamination failure modes — microbial, particulate, and pyrogen — and the environmental controls (ISO classification, pressure differentials, HEPA filtration) designed to prevent them. Compounding safety is especially relevant to me because I plan to pursue a career in hospital pharmacy, where sterile compounding is performed daily. The history of outbreaks and patient deaths from contaminated compounded products makes clear that lapses in aseptic technique are not just quality problems — they are patient safety crises. I need to be technically excellent at sterile compounding before I am ever responsible for a patient. I will practice aseptic technique during any lab sessions available and review USP 797 in full before my sterile compounding rotation.

We discussed pharmaceutical compounding safety in class today, including relevant regulatory standards and some case examples of compounding failures. I was deeply affected by the discussion of patients who received contaminated compounded medications in clinical settings — situations where they were entirely dependent on the healthcare system and its quality controls to protect them. It struck me that patients receiving compounded sterile preparations often have no way to evaluate the quality of what they receive. That dependency creates a profound responsibility for the pharmacist. During my compounding lab and any compounding rotations, I will treat every step of the aseptic technique as non-negotiable, practice garbing and hood technique consistently, and ask to observe environmental monitoring procedures so I understand how the site confirms its cleanroom standards.

Today's class covered compounding safety comprehensively, reviewing USP 795 and 797 standards, the types of compounding errors (calculation errors, contamination, incorrect ingredient substitution), and the regulatory framework that governs both community and hospital compounding. We reviewed the New England Compounding Center outbreak in detail, including the cascade of regulatory failures that allowed it to occur. Compounding safety is a critical area for pharmacists to understand. The consequences of errors can be catastrophic on a public health scale. I am still thinking about specific applications of this learning to my own practice.

Today's class was about compounding safety and the standards that apply to non-sterile and sterile preparations. We discussed some famous compounding failures and what pharmacists can do to prevent them. Pharmaceutical compounding involves significant risk if not done correctly, and USP standards exist for good reason. I will take compounding labs and rotations seriously and follow all protocols carefully.
We covered some pharmacy compounding topics today. I will make sure to follow the rules and procedures when I do any compounding at my sites.
Class today covered compounding in pharmacy. There are important standards that need to be followed to keep patients safe. I will study more about these requirements.

In today's session, we covered evidence-based patient counseling strategies for medication safety, including health literacy-sensitive communication, teach-back methodology, and the use of standardized counseling mnemonics like the Indian Health Service Prime Questions. We also reviewed how language barriers affect medication adherence and error rates, and the pharmacist's obligation to use professional interpreter services rather than relying on family members or gestures. A simulation exercise required us to counsel a standardized patient with limited health literacy on starting warfarin, and I found it significantly more difficult than expected. The simulation revealed something about my assumptions I had not recognized before: I tend to assume patients understand more than they do, particularly around concepts like international normalized ratio, drug-food interactions, and the need for frequent monitoring. I over-used technical language even when I thought I was simplifying. This session made me see patient counseling not as a checklist item at the end of a dispensing workflow, but as a core safety intervention — because an informed, empowered patient is one of the most effective safeguards against adverse drug events. After each counseling encounter at my internship, I will use the teach-back method to confirm understanding by asking the patient to explain the key points back to me in their own words. If a patient indicates any language preference other than English, I will request a professional interpreter before proceeding with counseling, even under time pressure.

This week's class focused on patient counseling, reviewing communication techniques, health literacy considerations, and the regulatory requirement (OBRA 90) for counseling offers to Medicaid patients. We practiced using the IHS Prime Questions framework and discussed how counseling serves as a final safety check — an opportunity for the patient to catch errors the pharmacy might have missed. We also reviewed studies showing that effective pharmacist counseling reduces hospitalization rates and adverse drug events. Patient counseling is important, and I understand it is both a legal requirement and a safety intervention. I plan to proactively offer counseling to every new prescription patient during my retail pharmacy rotation, including using teach-back to confirm understanding of high-risk medications like anticoagulants, insulin, and narrow therapeutic index drugs.

We discussed patient counseling techniques in class today. The content included some communication frameworks and health literacy considerations. As a first-generation immigrant, I grew up watching my family members struggle to communicate with healthcare providers, and the section on interpreter services resonated with me deeply. I saw my aunt misuse an inhaler for years because she was too embarrassed to ask for help and the pharmacist had not noticed or addressed the language barrier. Helping patients from similar backgrounds access safe, clear medication information feels like a personal mission for me. I will ask my preceptors at every site whether interpreter services are available and actively use them whenever needed. I will also practice teach-back in every counseling session and note any cases where a patient's understanding was different from what I expected.

In class, we reviewed counseling frameworks including the IHS Prime Questions, teach-back methodology, and communication adaptations for patients with low health literacy or language barriers. We discussed the evidence linking effective counseling to reduced adverse drug events and improved adherence outcomes. We practiced abbreviated counseling scenarios in pairs. I have not yet developed a strong personal connection to this topic, though I recognize its clinical importance. I will practice counseling skills at my internship and request feedback from my preceptor on my communication technique.

We discussed patient counseling in class, including how pharmacists communicate medication information effectively. We reviewed health literacy, language barriers, and the importance of confirming patient understanding. Good counseling is a pharmacist responsibility that protects patients from medication errors. I will make an effort to counsel patients clearly and check their understanding at my internship.
Class today was about counseling patients on their medications. This is an important part of the pharmacist's role in ensuring patients are safe. I will work on improving my communication skills.
We covered pharmacy counseling today. I will practice talking to patients at my internship and try to make sure they understand how to take their medications.

This week's session covered opioid safety comprehensively — morphine milligram equivalents (MME), the CDC prescribing guidelines for chronic pain, risks of combining opioids with benzodiazepines, opioid-induced respiratory depression, and the critical role of naloxone distribution in the community. We reviewed the trajectory of the opioid epidemic, including how prescribing practices drove a rise in iatrogenic opioid dependence before transitioning into illicit fentanyl-driven overdose deaths. We discussed pharmacist-specific roles: dispensing naloxone under standing orders, reviewing MME thresholds, checking PDMPs, and counseling patients on safe storage and disposal. This topic carries personal weight for me. A close friend struggled with opioid use disorder that began after a sports injury, and watching how the healthcare system both created and failed to address that problem shaped my view of pharmaceutical harm. This session gave me a framework to understand what went wrong systemically and how pharmacists could have intervened differently at multiple points — from initial prescribing to community-level naloxone access. I am not someone who will be passive about this. At my community pharmacy rotation, I will ask to observe naloxone dispensing counseling sessions and eventually conduct them myself. I will review the PDMP for every Schedule II opioid prescription I help process and flag any MME thresholds over 90 MME per day for preceptor review.

In class today, we covered opioid prescribing safety — MME calculation, combined opioid-benzodiazepine risk, naloxone co-prescription, and community pharmacist responsibilities during the opioid epidemic. We reviewed the 2022 CDC Clinical Practice Guideline and how its recommendations differ from prior guidance. We discussed specific counseling points for naloxone including device training for different formulations (intranasal vs. auto-injector), when to use it, and how to call 911. Opioid safety is not an abstract topic for me — I grew up in a rural county that was heavily affected by the epidemic, and I have seen the consequences in my community. This class gave me a more systemic perspective on how pharmacy-level interventions — particularly PDMP checks, MME monitoring, and naloxone co-prescription — can make a measurable difference at scale. I will make naloxone counseling a priority at every relevant encounter during my rotations and ask my preceptor to evaluate my technique.

Today we focused on opioid safety, including the pharmacology of opioid-induced respiratory depression, MME calculation, the risk of combining opioids with CNS depressants, and harm reduction strategies including naloxone. We reviewed PDMP policies across different states and practiced calculating MME for a patient on multiple opioid formulations. Opioid safety is clearly an important area of pharmacy practice. I will apply what I learned by calculating MME for any opioid prescription I verify during my rotations and will offer naloxone co-prescription counseling whenever appropriate. I will also review the PDMP before dispensing any Schedule II opioid.

We covered opioid safety today, including some calculation methods and safety guidelines. The discussion of naloxone was helpful. This topic matters to me because I want to work in a community pharmacy setting, and I know that opioid safety counseling will be part of my daily work. Seeing statistics about how many opioid overdose deaths are preventable with naloxone reinforced my belief that pharmacists can save lives through active patient engagement — not just medication dispensing. I will practice naloxone counseling at my retail rotation and ask my preceptor for feedback.

In today's class, we reviewed opioid safety in depth — including the pharmacokinetics of common opioids, MME thresholds from CDC guidelines, risks of polypharmacy with CNS depressants, and naloxone's mechanism and dispensing protocols. We practiced using the MME calculator with a complex patient case involving both a long-acting and short-acting opioid. I am still forming my personal perspective on this topic. I will try to apply MME awareness during my rotations.

We discussed opioid safety in class, covering naloxone, MME calculation, and the pharmacist's role in the opioid epidemic. We reviewed relevant guidelines and how community pharmacists can help reduce harm. Opioid safety is one of the most urgent public health issues in pharmacy practice. I will try to follow safe dispensing practices and counsel patients on naloxone when appropriate during my internship.
Today's class covered opioids and their safety considerations. Pharmacists have an important role in preventing harm from opioid medications. I will learn more about this topic.
We covered opioid-related content in class today. I will make sure to ask my preceptor about the opioid-related procedures at my site and try to learn from real cases during my rotation.

In today's class, we covered renal dose adjustment principles in depth — the use of the Cockcroft-Gault equation to estimate creatinine clearance, the difference between GFR-based and CrCl-based dosing decisions, and the drugs most commonly requiring adjustment in renal impairment. We reviewed specific examples including vancomycin, metformin, digoxin, and enoxaparin, and discussed how failure to adjust doses in dialysis patients has resulted in drug accumulation and serious toxicity. We also covered the nuances of serum creatinine as a marker — its limitations in elderly patients with low muscle mass, in patients with acute kidney injury, and in those on dialysis. I was surprised to learn how frequently renal dose adjustments are missed in practice, even in hospital settings with robust electronic clinical decision support. This session challenged my assumption that electronic alerts reliably catch these errors. I now understand that CDS alerts require accurate patient data (weight, creatinine, indication) to fire correctly, and that those inputs are often missing or stale. This means pharmacists cannot rely on the system — we have to know the underlying principles. At my clinical rotation, I will calculate CrCl independently for every patient I counsel on a renally-cleared medication rather than relying solely on the EHR-generated recommendation. I will flag any cases where the EHR value differs from my calculation and discuss the discrepancy with my preceptor.

Today we reviewed renal dose adjustment with a focus on calculating creatinine clearance, interpreting GFR stages, and applying dose modification recommendations from references like Lexicomp and the drug package insert. We reviewed cases of metformin-associated lactic acidosis in patients with unrecognized renal impairment and digoxin toxicity from inappropriate dosing in an elderly patient. Renal dose adjustment is critically important because the consequences of getting it wrong — toxicity or therapeutic failure — are often irreversible, especially in patients with already-compromised renal function. I think about the elderly patients in my community pharmacy who are frail and have reduced muscle mass, meaning their serum creatinine may look normal but their actual renal function may be severely diminished. I need to be the one who catches this. I have not yet fully worked out how I will apply this in practice, but I know I need to make renal function review a standard part of my patient assessment.

We discussed renal dose adjustment in class, reviewing the Cockcroft-Gault equation and some key drug examples. I feel personally motivated by this topic because I cared for a family member with end-stage renal disease who experienced a preventable drug toxicity when a physician did not adjust her antibiotic dose. The pharmacist later identified the error, but only after she had been experiencing symptoms for several days. This experience made me aware of how critical pharmacist review of renal function is in complex patients. I will calculate CrCl for every renally-cleared drug I encounter during rotations and compare my result to the EHR value. I will also ask my preceptor to review the site's process for identifying patients with undocumented or fluctuating renal function.

In today's session, we covered renal pharmacokinetics and dose adjustment strategies, including the Cockcroft-Gault equation, GFR staging, and drug-specific adjustment recommendations. We also discussed the unique challenges of dosing in patients receiving hemodialysis or continuous renal replacement therapy. Renal dose adjustment is a clinically important skill and I understand that errors in this area can cause serious harm. I will keep this in mind when reviewing medication orders during clinical rotations.

We covered renal dosing today including CrCl calculations and some drug examples. Patient safety is important in this area since kidney impairment is common and many drugs require adjusted doses. I will review renal function when dispensing medications during my rotations.
Class today covered kidney-related drug dosing. I will practice creatinine clearance calculations and remember to check renal function for high-risk drugs at my site.
We discussed drug dosing and kidney function in class. This is clearly an important area for clinical pharmacists to understand. I will pay attention to this in practice.

Today's session focused on transitions of care pharmacy — the high-risk period when patients move between care settings such as from hospital to home, hospital to skilled nursing facility, or emergency department to outpatient. We reviewed the evidence base for pharmacist-led discharge interventions, including the PILL study and Project Re-Engineered Discharge (RED), both of which demonstrated significant reductions in 30-day readmissions when pharmacists were involved in discharge counseling and medication reconciliation. We also discussed the typical discharge medication error profile: omitted medications, dose changes not communicated, new drug-drug interactions from combined inpatient and outpatient regimens, and inadequate patient education about medication changes. This topic helped me connect several concepts from prior sessions — medication reconciliation, patient counseling, drug interactions — into an integrated model of the care transition process. I now see the discharge interaction as one of the highest-stakes moments in pharmacy practice and one of the areas where pharmacists are most underpresent relative to the evidence for their impact. At my hospital rotation, I will ask to participate in at least three pharmacist-led discharge counseling sessions, focusing on patients who had medication changes during hospitalization. I will document the number of discrepancies or education gaps I identify and debrief with my preceptor after each encounter.

In class, we reviewed transitions of care concepts, including the care transition model, common discharge medication errors, and evidence-based pharmacy interventions like the MTM discharge bundle. We discussed why the hospital-to-home transition is so high-risk: patients are often cognitively fatigued, managing multiple new medications, and unclear about what changed and why. We also reviewed CMS quality measures related to readmissions and how pharmacist involvement affects them. Transitions of care safety is clearly important, and pharmacists are well-positioned to intervene. During my hospital rotation, I will ask to observe discharge counseling sessions, paying particular attention to how pharmacists explain medication changes to patients in plain language, and I will practice these conversations myself under supervision.

We discussed transitions of care in class today, reviewing some statistics on readmissions and the role of pharmacists. This topic resonated with me because my mother was readmitted to the hospital within two weeks of a prior discharge after she ran out of a cardiac medication because the discharge prescription was never sent to the pharmacy. The admitting physician was surprised, but no one had ensured the prescription was transmitted and filled. I can see now that the pharmacist at the outpatient pharmacy could have identified the gap if there had been a formal transition of care handoff. I plan to ask about the hospital-to-community pharmacy communication processes at each of my rotation sites and think about where I can intervene most effectively.

Today's class covered transitions of care in detail, reviewing the epidemiology of medication-related readmissions, specific error types at discharge, and pharmacist-led intervention models. We reviewed the PILL study protocol, which involved pharmacist-conducted counseling, medication reconciliation, and 72-hour follow-up calls, and how it reduced readmissions by 18%. Understanding transitions of care makes me think about the real costs of gaps in pharmacy services. I want to be the pharmacist who closes those gaps. I am still thinking about how to translate this into specific actions at my upcoming rotation.

Class today covered transitions of care and how medication errors commonly occur when patients move between settings. We discussed the pharmacist's role in preventing these errors. Transitions of care are a high-risk period for patients, and pharmacists have an important role in preventing medication-related readmissions. I will look for opportunities to participate in discharge counseling during my hospital rotation.
We talked about how patients' medications can get mixed up when they move from one place to another. I will try to participate in discharge counseling at my rotation site when I have the chance.
Today we discussed care transitions and medication safety. This is an important area where pharmacists can make a real difference. I want to learn more about how this works in practice.

This session focused on root cause analysis (RCA) as a patient safety methodology, including its theoretical basis in just culture, the difference between RCA and blame-based error investigation, and the structured fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram approach to identifying contributing factors. We reviewed a case of a wrong-dose dispensing error and walked through the complete RCA process — from identifying the immediate cause to uncovering the latent system conditions that made the error possible: inadequate lighting, lack of a second-check policy, a poorly designed label template, and staffing shortages on the day the error occurred. The RCA framework transformed how I think about pharmacy errors. Before today, I would have attributed most errors to individual carelessness. The systematic analysis showed me that individual errors almost always occur at the intersection of multiple system vulnerabilities, and that blaming the individual does nothing to fix those underlying conditions — meaning the same error will happen again. This realization makes me want to be someone who does not just avoid errors personally, but who actively advocates for system improvements. During my internship, when I observe a near miss or error, I will attempt to do a brief informal RCA in my head — identifying at least three contributing factors beyond the individual — and share those observations with my preceptor rather than just noting that an error occurred.

In today's class, we covered the root cause analysis framework, including the Swiss Cheese Model revisited in the context of structured RCA, the difference between proximate causes and root causes, and how healthcare organizations use RCA reports to drive systemic improvement. We reviewed a hospital case where a patient received a tenfold heparin overdose and practiced identifying root causes using a fishbone diagram. We also discussed the psychological safety required for honest RCA and how just culture principles facilitate reporting. RCA is an important tool for improving safety. I plan to approach any errors or near misses I observe during rotations with a root cause mindset — identifying systemic factors rather than stopping at individual blame. I will also review my rotation site's error reporting procedures and ask my preceptor how completed RCAs are used to drive changes at their organization.

We covered root cause analysis in class and went through the basic method using a case example. The topic resonated with me because during a previous internship, a dispensing error occurred and the response from the manager was to criticize the technician publicly rather than investigate why the error happened. That approach felt wrong at the time, and this class gave me the framework to understand why — blame-focused responses suppress reporting and leave the system conditions intact. I am committed to supporting just culture in any organization I work in. During my rotations, I will model the behavior of identifying systemic contributing factors when errors occur and advocate for reporting rather than concealment when I observe near misses.

Today's class walked through root cause analysis as a structured patient safety method, covering the fishbone diagram, the five whys technique, and the distinction between proximate and root causes. We reviewed case examples from pharmacy and nursing and discussed how RCA reports are used by pharmacy quality departments to drive system improvements. The concept that resonated most was systems thinking — the idea that individual errors are predictable outputs of flawed systems, not aberrations caused by bad people. I want to be a pharmacist who approaches errors with curiosity rather than judgment. I am still figuring out how to translate this into concrete actions during my rotations.

We discussed root cause analysis and how it is used to investigate errors in pharmacy and healthcare. We went through a case and identified different contributing factors. RCA is a valuable safety tool because it focuses on system improvement rather than individual blame, which leads to better long-term outcomes. I will try to apply a root cause mindset when I encounter errors or near misses during my training.

Today we talked about how errors are investigated in healthcare settings. It seems like there is more to errors than just one person making a mistake. I will keep this in mind when I encounter any problems during my rotations.
Class today covered safety investigation methods. I will try to think about the bigger picture whenever I notice a problem at my internship site.

Today's class covered medication error disclosure — the ethical and legal framework for communicating errors to patients and families, the communication techniques used in disclosure conversations, and the evidence showing that transparent disclosure actually reduces malpractice claims rather than increasing them. We reviewed the CANDOR toolkit and practiced using it in a simulated disclosure scenario where a patient had received an incorrect dose of a blood pressure medication for two weeks. The simulation revealed how uncomfortable I am with uncertainty and apology. I found myself wanting to qualify everything, minimize the significance, and avoid saying directly that an error had occurred. This is exactly the opposite of what effective disclosure requires. Understanding this discomfort — and recognizing it as a clinical skill gap — was valuable. I now understand that disclosure is not just the right thing to do ethically; it is also the most effective way to preserve the therapeutic relationship and address patient harm. At my next rotation, I will ask my preceptor whether they have been involved in a disclosure conversation and whether I might observe one if appropriate. I will also practice delivering a structured apology using the CANDOR framework with a peer before I encounter a real situation.

In class, we covered error disclosure from both ethical and practical perspectives, reviewing the legal landscape around apology laws, the CANDOR toolkit, and the psychological impact of disclosure on patients and healthcare providers. We went through a case study where a patient received a wrong drug for three days and the healthcare team debated whether and how to disclose the error. We also discussed second victim syndrome — the emotional harm experienced by the clinician who made the error. Error disclosure is a difficult but necessary skill for pharmacists to develop. I plan to practice disclosure communication in simulation settings whenever possible and ask my preceptor to review the site's disclosure policy with me.

We discussed medication error disclosure in class today. The legal and ethical framework was complex, and the simulated exercise was challenging. This topic connects to something I believe in strongly — patient autonomy. Patients have the right to know what happened to them, even when the news is difficult to deliver. I have read about cases where errors were concealed from patients and later discovered, causing far more damage to trust than honest disclosure would have. I want to be someone who acts with integrity in those moments. I plan to learn the disclosure process at my rotation sites and be willing to participate in those conversations when appropriate.

Today's session covered medication error disclosure, including the CANDOR framework, apology law protections, institutional disclosure policies, and the second victim experience. We practiced structured disclosure in pairs, which was surprisingly difficult — the challenge of being clear about what happened while also being compassionate was not easy to balance. This topic matters deeply because patients who are harmed by medical errors deserve honesty. Research shows that patients primarily want acknowledgment and an apology — not litigation — and that concealment or deflection makes outcomes worse for everyone. I understand intellectually that disclosure is the right approach, but I am still working on the emotional readiness to do it well in practice.

We went over the topic of medication error disclosure, including how it should be done and why transparency is important. We did a practice exercise that was challenging. Disclosure is an important professional responsibility, and patients deserve to know when errors have occurred. I will learn more about the disclosure policies at my rotation sites.
Today we talked about how to tell patients when a medication error has occurred. This seems like a very difficult thing to do but is clearly important. I will work on developing this skill.
Class today was about communicating with patients after an error. I will ask my preceptor about any disclosure procedures at my site and try to learn how this is handled.

Today's class covered pharmaceutical storage standards, including USP temperature classifications, requirements for controlled room temperature, refrigerated, and frozen medications, and the safety implications of temperature excursions for different drug classes. We reviewed the cold chain requirements for vaccines and biologics, including insulin, and discussed how breaks in the cold chain — whether in shipping, pharmacy storage, or patient handling — can result in reduced potency or product failure. We also covered light-sensitive medications, segregation requirements for hazardous drugs, and the regulatory implications of dispensing an expired medication. I had not fully appreciated how much drug storage affects drug safety before this session. I grew up in a household where my parents kept all their medications in the bathroom medicine cabinet, which is actually one of the worst storage environments due to humidity and temperature fluctuations. This session made me realize that improper storage is not just a technical compliance issue — it is a patient safety issue that I, as a pharmacist, have a responsibility to address at the point of dispensing. At every counseling opportunity, I will include storage instructions as a standard counseling point, not just for temperature-sensitive medications but also for those affected by humidity or light. I will also audit the medication storage areas at my rotation sites and report any temperature excursion logs or improper segregation I observe to my preceptor.

In class today, we covered drug storage requirements in detail — USP temperature ranges, refrigerated storage monitoring, cold chain requirements for biologics, and the storage requirements for controlled substances. We also reviewed the consequences of storage failures: reduced drug potency, degradation products with toxic potential, and regulatory violations. Case examples included a community pharmacy that had used a malfunctioning refrigerator for insulin for several weeks without noticing the temperature log had been out of range. Proper drug storage is an area I had underestimated before. Hearing about the insulin case made me think about how patients rely on the pharmacy to ensure the medications they receive are stored properly — they have no way to verify that themselves. I will make storage counseling a standard part of my practice and will ask to review temperature monitoring logs at my rotation sites.

Today we reviewed drug storage standards and the safety implications of improper storage. There were several important points about cold chain management and temperature excursions. I feel a personal connection to this topic because I have worked in a retail pharmacy where I noticed that some insulin pens in the display case were close to their expiration date and had been stored near the door seal, which allowed intermittent temperature fluctuations. I did not say anything at the time, not knowing what the protocol was. This class made me realize that I had identified a genuine safety issue and that speaking up was not only appropriate but required. At my internship, I will check temperature logs and medication storage conditions as part of my regular responsibilities, and I will report concerns about potential temperature excursions to my supervisor promptly.

Today's session covered pharmaceutical storage requirements including USP storage condition classifications, monitoring procedures for refrigerators and freezers, and the handling requirements for light-sensitive medications. We also discussed hazardous drug storage requirements under USP 800 and the segregation requirements for cytotoxic agents. Drug storage is an often-overlooked aspect of medication safety, and I now have a clearer picture of how storage failures can compromise drug integrity and ultimately harm patients. I will pay attention to storage standards at my sites.

We went over drug storage standards in class today. We covered temperature ranges, cold chain management, and some examples of storage failures. Proper drug storage is important for maintaining medication efficacy and patient safety. I will review storage conditions and monitoring logs at my rotation sites.
Today's class was about storing medications properly. I will pay attention to storage conditions and temperature logs at my internship and ask questions if anything seems off.

We talked about how important it is to store medications correctly. This is something pharmacists need to monitor carefully to ensure patient safety. I will study more about the specific requirements.

In today's session, we covered immunization safety comprehensively — Vaccine Information Statements (VIS), contraindications and precautions, VAERS reporting requirements, anaphylaxis management protocols, vaccine schedule decision-making, and cold chain management. We also discussed the pharmacist's expanding role in administering vaccines and the responsibility to screen patients for contraindications and potential adverse reactions. A simulation exercise tested our ability to assess a patient with an egg allergy for influenza vaccine eligibility using current ACIP guidelines. Before this session, I viewed immunization as a fairly straightforward pharmacist activity — confirm age, give the shot, document. The simulation revealed how much clinical judgment is actually required: interpreting allergy histories, distinguishing contraindications from precautions, recognizing early signs of anaphylaxis, and managing the epinephrine kit in a real emergency. I was also confronted with how important counseling is in the immunization context, particularly for patients who have vaccine hesitancy — not to argue with them, but to have an informed, respectful conversation. At my community pharmacy rotation, I will review the current ACIP schedule before each shift, ask to participate in immunization consultations, and practice anaphylaxis management scenarios with my preceptor to ensure I am prepared to respond if a reaction occurs.

Today's class covered vaccine safety, including VAERS reporting, Vaccine Information Statements, anaphylaxis recognition and management, and the ACIP immunization schedule. We also discussed contraindications to common vaccines, including MMR, varicella, and live vaccines in immunocompromised patients. We practiced administering IM injections using simulation models and reviewed the two-minute post-vaccination observation requirement. This topic deepened my appreciation for the precision required in immunization practice. A single oversight — giving a live vaccine to an immunocompromised patient, missing a contraindication, failing to keep epinephrine available — can cause serious harm. I also think about the impact at a population level: pharmacists administer millions of vaccines annually, and the quality of our screening and administration directly affects herd immunity. I will develop a personal screening checklist for common vaccines and ask to perform supervised vaccine administrations at my retail rotation.

We covered immunization safety in class today, including vaccine adverse events, contraindications, and the pharmacist's role in immunization programs. As someone who has family members who have expressed vaccine hesitancy, this topic felt particularly relevant to me. I want to be able to have compassionate, evidence-based conversations about vaccine safety concerns rather than dismissive ones. The section on VAERS and how adverse events are tracked reinforced for me that the surveillance system exists because safety is taken seriously — and that reporting adverse events is a professional obligation, not optional. During my rotations, I will participate in vaccine administration sessions and practice using VIS documents with patients. I will also review VAERS reporting procedures at each site so I know how to submit a report if needed.

Today we covered immunization safety in detail, including the pharmacist immunizer certification requirements, ACIP scheduling guidelines, contraindication screening protocols, anaphylaxis management, and VAERS reporting. We reviewed several case examples including inappropriate vaccine administration in immunocompromised patients. Immunization safety is an important area of pharmacist practice as we administer more vaccines than any other healthcare provider group. I recognize the clinical responsibility this entails. I will keep these safety principles in mind during my rotations.
